text: Chapter 13 teaches cycle-of-fifths practice as a foundational technique for building familiarity with all twelve keys and smooth harmonic movement, or voice leading. The chapter contrasts root-position playing (which works but sounds clunky) with voice-leading approaches that move voices by half-step, whole-step, or mixed intervals, emphasizing the core conceptual shift from thinking of chords as static dots to thinking of harmonic movement as several moving voices. Key techniques include contrary motion for V7-I progressions, understanding tritone pull (the unresolved Bb and E being pulled toward A and F in resolution), and the spread texture, where bass notes alternate up a fourth and down a fifth to create a tenth interval from the melody above. The master's characteristic moves visible here are the voice-by-voice breakdowns (such as the C7-to-F9 analysis) that reveal how each voice contributes to smooth, airy harmonic movement rather than parallel root motion. Load-bearing terminology introduced includes voice leading, harmonic rhythm, tritone pull, and spread as a fingerstyle arrangement texture.
